meta.name: Waakye
meta.subtitle: Ghanaian rice and beans cooked with waakye leaves (or safe substitutes)

source.name: ssa
notes: Canonical SSA waakye base recipe: rice + black-eyed peas (or cowpeas) cooked with dried waakye leaves (sorghum leaves) for color and aroma.
Includes safe substitution guidance for hard-to-find waakye leaves and optional traditional sides (stew, gari, shito, eggs, fish).

summary: Hearty Ghanaian rice-and-beans staple traditionally tinted by dried waakye leaves—served with stew, gari, shito, and add-ons like egg or fish.

yield.notes: Base waakye (rice + beans). Add-ons can stretch servings further.

signals.makeAhead: Cook waakye base 1–3 days ahead; reheat well with a splash of water/steam.

signatureNotes: comforting, neutral base for spicy stews

ingredients.label: Dried black-eyed peas (cowpeas)
notes: Traditional waakye is commonly made with cowpeas/black-eyed peas.
Substitute: small red beans or black beans (cook time may increase).

ingredients.label: Dried waakye leaves (sorghum leaves)
notes: These provide the characteristic reddish-brown tint and subtle aroma.
If unavailable, use the substitution options below.

ingredients.label: Baking soda (optional helper for color/softening)
notes: Optional and small. Too much can make beans mushy and taste soapy.
Use only if beans are older/tough or if using substitutions for leaves.

ingredients.label: Long-grain rice (parboiled or jasmine)
notes: Parboiled rice holds up well and stays separate.
Rinse rice well to reduce starchiness.

ingredients.label: Onion, halved
ingredients.notes: Adds gentle flavor. Can be removed before serving.

ingredients.label: Salt
ingredients.notes: Start here; adjust based on toppings (stew/shito can be salty).

ingredients.label: Water
notes: Used in stages. You may not use it all; depends on bean age and simmer rate.

ingredients.label: Shito (Ghanaian black pepper sauce), for serving
ingredients.notes: Traditional spicy condiment; serve on the side.

ingredients.label: Ghana-style tomato stew, for serving
ingredients.notes: Often served with stew and/or protein.

ingredients.label: Smoked or fried fish, for serving
ingredients.notes: Optional but traditional; serve alongside waakye with stew/shito.

steps.title: Sort and rinse the beans
instructions: Pick through dried black-eyed peas to remove any debris or damaged beans.
Rinse thoroughly under cool water until the water runs mostly clear.

steps.title: Start cooking the beans (with waakye leaves if available)
instructions: Add rinsed beans to a pot with 6 cups of water.
If using dried waakye leaves: rinse the leaves quickly, then add them to the pot (tie in clean kitchen twine or place in a tea infuser bag if you want easy removal).
Add halved onion (optional).
Bring to a boil, then reduce to a steady simmer.
checks: Pot reaches a steady simmer without boiling over.

steps.title: Simmer beans until mostly tender
instructions: Simmer beans partially covered until they are mostly tender (typically 30–45 minutes, depending on bean age).
If water drops below the bean level, add hot water as needed (use the remaining 2 cups).
Optional: If beans are very old/tough, add baking soda (1/4 tsp) after 20 minutes to help soften (do not exceed 1/2 tsp total).
checks: Beans are mostly tender but not falling apart.
Cooking liquid has taken on a light reddish-brown tint if using leaves.

steps.title: Remove leaves/onion (optional) and season the pot
instructions: If used, remove waakye leaves and discard (or remove the tea bag/infuser).
Remove onion halves if you prefer a cleaner base (or chop and leave in).
Stir in salt.
checks: Seasoning is added; beans remain intact.

steps.title: Rinse rice and add to the beans
instructions: Rinse rice in several changes of water until it runs clearer (reduces stickiness).
Add rinsed rice to the pot with the beans.
Add enough water so the liquid level sits about 3/4 inch above the rice-and-beans mixture (usually 2.5–3 cups of liquid total in the pot at this stage).
Bring to a gentle boil, then reduce to very low heat and cover tightly.
checks: Rice is rinsed; pot is covered and at low heat to steam.

steps.title: Steam until rice is tender
instructions: Cook on very low heat until rice is tender and liquid is absorbed (about 18–22 minutes).
Avoid stirring while the rice cooks (prevents gumminess).
If the pot seems dry before rice is tender, add 2–4 tablespoons water, cover, and continue steaming.
checks: Rice is tender; no pooled liquid remains at the bottom.

steps.title: Rest and fluff
instructions: Turn off heat and let the pot rest covered for 8–10 minutes.
Fluff gently with a fork or spoon, lifting from the bottom to mix beans and rice evenly.
checks: Waakye is fluffy, not mushy; beans are evenly distributed.

steps.title: Serve with traditional toppings (optional but classic)
instructions: Serve waakye hot as a base on plates/bowls.
Top or side with tomato stew, shito, and a sprinkle of gari if using.
Add hard-boiled egg and/or fish if desired.
checks: Seasoning is balanced and toppings are added to preference.

adaptations.title: If waakye leaves are not available
notes: Waakye leaves primarily contribute color and a subtle earthy aroma.
Choose ONE substitution path below for a similar look (flavor will differ).

options.label: Black tea color method (visual substitute)
options.how: Steep 2 black tea bags in 2 cups boiling water for 5 minutes. Use this tea as part of the cooking liquid for beans. Remove bags before adding rice.

options.label: Dried hibiscus (sobolo/zobo) tint (lightly fruity)
options.how: Add 1–2 tbsp dried hibiscus during bean simmer for 10 minutes, then remove. Use lightly—can add tang.

options.label: Color via grain blend (more traditional-ish look)
options.how: Replace 1/2 cup of the white rice with brown rice or red rice; expect longer cook time or par-cook brown rice first.

fridge.notes: Cool quickly, store covered. Reheat with a splash of water and steam.

freezer.notes: Freeze in flat portions. Thaw overnight and steam to reheat.

reheat.notes: Best reheated by steaming: add 1–2 tbsp water, cover, low heat 6–10 minutes, fluff.

notes: Cool rice/beans promptly for food safety (do not leave at room temp for long periods).
Reheat to steaming hot; refrigerate leftovers within 2 hours.
Bean age matters. If beans are taking too long, soak 6–8 hours or overnight next time (drain and cook in fresh water).
If using parboiled rice, waakye stays fluffy and holds well for leftovers.
Salt early enough to season the rice, but consider salty toppings like shito/stew.

variations.label: Pressure cooker / Instant Pot method (faster beans)

adapt.notes: Pressure-cook beans (with leaves if using) ~10–12 minutes with natural release, then add rice and cook on low pressure 4 minutes with 10-minute natural release. Liquid amounts must be adjusted to your cooker’s minimums.
Use brown rice; par-cook beans fully, then cook brown rice separately or extend the steaming time substantially. Keep beans from overcooking by combining near the end.
